title: Making potting compost from your own pile
subtitle: Ratios for seed compost, general potting mix, and raised-bed filling using mature garden compost
excerpt: Mature garden compost on its own is too dense and nutrient-rich for seed germination, but blended with sharp sand or perlite it makes a capable seed compost. For general potting use and raised-bed topping, a 40-50% compost blend with topsoil, grit, and organic matter works well. The key is maturity: half-rotted compost causes damping off in seedlings.
sourceNotes: RHS guide to making your own compost and potting mixes. Charles Dowding growing without digging for compost as mulch and growing medium. Garden Organic seed-starting guidance. Gardeners World magazine compost blend recommendations. APHA (Animal and Plant Health Agency) guidance on sourcing and using garden compost for food growing.

content.text: Mature garden compost improves soil and makes a good mulch. Used straight from the pile, it is too dense and rich for seed germination. High nitrogen causes salt stress in germinating seeds and encourages damping-off fungi. Blended with inert material, it becomes a capable growing medium for most purposes. The critical requirement is maturity: the compost must be fully broken down, dark brown to black, crumbly, and smelling of earth.
Testing compost maturity
Smell: earthy, not ammonia or rotten. Ammonia means still-active breakdown; rotten smell means anaerobic conditions.
Structure: crumbly, with no visible food scraps or large un-decomposed plant material.
Germination test: fill a small pot with the compost and sow 10 cress seeds. If 8 or more germinate within 7 days, the compost is mature. Cress is sensitive to immature compost toxins.
Seed compost blend
For germinating seeds and growing young seedlings up to the first true-leaf stage:
50% mature compost (sieved through a 5 mm riddle)
50% sharp sand or perlite (not builder's sand, which is too fine and compacts)
This blend is lower-nutrient, free-draining, and fine-textured enough for small seeds. It will not sustain plants beyond the first true leaf stage without feeding.
General potting mix
For potting on seedlings, containers, and growing bags:
40% mature compost
40% topsoil (loam-based, if available) or bought peat-free compost
20% sharp sand or perlite for drainage
This mix keeps hungry plants like tomatoes and courgettes going through the season. Start liquid feeding by midsummer.
Raised-bed filling
For a raised bed being filled or topped up after a season:
50% topsoil or existing bed soil
50% mature compost as a top 100-150 mm mulch layer
Put compost on top of the bed in autumn or spring. Leave it there: worms pull it down. A thick layer each year is enough.
